questions.q: In the Raft consensus algorithm, what is the primary role of the leader?
options: To replicate log entries to followers and commit entries when a majority acknowledges them
To ensure every node has identical memory at all times
To resolve Byzantine faults by majority voting on transaction hashes
To eliminate the need for followers to store any data
questions.explanation: The leader in Raft replicates its log to followers and commits entries once a majority has acknowledged them, ensuring consensus. Option 1 is incorrect because Raft doesn't require identical memory—it tolerates temporary inconsistencies. Option 2 confuses Raft with Byzantine fault tolerance algorithms.

questions.q: What is a quorum in distributed consensus?
options: A voting block that represents the majority of nodes required to make a decision
A predetermined set of nodes chosen at random for each consensus round
The total number of nodes in the system minus one
A mechanism that prevents any node from voting twice
questions.explanation: A quorum is the minimum number of nodes (typically a majority) whose agreement is necessary for a decision to be valid. Option 1 is a misconception about random selection. Option 2 is incorrect because quorum size is fixed, not random.

questions.q: In Paxos, what is the purpose of the Proposer role?
options: To initiate consensus by proposing values and handling retries when proposals are rejected
To store all proposed values and reject duplicates
To verify that all Acceptors have identical state
To broadcast messages directly to Learners without going through Acceptors
questions.explanation: A Proposer suggests values and must retry with higher proposal numbers if rejected. Option 1 is a function of Acceptors, not Proposers. Option 2 assumes Proposers bypass the Acceptor phase, which violates Paxos protocol.

questions.q: How many nodes can a Byzantine fault-tolerant system tolerate if it has n total nodes?
options: Up to (n-1)/3 faulty nodes
Up to n/2 faulty nodes
Up to n-1 faulty nodes
No faulty nodes—Byzantine systems require perfect reliability
questions.explanation: Byzantine fault tolerance requires that fewer than one-third of nodes are faulty to reach consensus. Option 1 is too permissive; systems with n/2 faulty nodes cannot guarantee consensus. Option 2 is false; Byzantine systems are designed specifically to tolerate some faults.

questions.q: In leader election, what is a split brain scenario?
options: Multiple nodes incorrectly believe themselves to be the leader, causing conflicting decisions
A situation where the leader node experiences memory corruption
When a follower node splits its data across multiple servers
A network partition that causes two nodes to communicate simultaneously
questions.explanation: Split brain occurs when two or more nodes independently claim leadership, violating the safety guarantee of consensus. Option 1 is about memory errors, not consensus. Option 2 is irrelevant to leadership.

questions.q: What property must a consensus algorithm guarantee to be considered safe?
options: All non-faulty nodes must eventually agree on the same value
Consensus must complete within a bounded time frame
Faulty nodes are immediately detected and removed
Every proposed value is guaranteed to be committed
questions.explanation: Safety in consensus requires agreement among all correct nodes on a single value. Option 1 is about liveness, not safety. Option 2 assumes faults are always detectable. Option 3 is false; not all proposals succeed.

questions.q: In Raft, what does the term 'election timeout' control?
options: How long a follower waits for a heartbeat before initiating a new leader election
The time a leader has to commit entries before relinquishing leadership
The duration a candidate remains in the running state before becoming a follower
The time allowed for a new leader to synchronize all follower logs
questions.explanation: The election timeout is the period during which followers expect heartbeats from the leader; if none arrive, they start an election. Option 1 confuses the timeout with the election process itself. Option 2 applies to the commitment process, not timing.

questions.q: Which characteristic distinguishes Raft from Paxos in terms of understandability?
options: Raft separates concerns into distinct phases (leader election, log replication, safety) explicitly
Paxos uses fewer state transitions, making it simpler to implement
Raft eliminates the need for any form of quorum agreements
Paxos guarantees liveness in all scenarios where Raft cannot
questions.explanation: Raft was designed with clarity in mind, clearly separating leader election from log replication. Option 1 is historically inaccurate; Paxos is known for being harder to understand. Option 2 falsely claims Raft eliminates quorum requirements.

questions.q: What happens in Raft when a candidate receives votes from a quorum of nodes?
options: The candidate immediately becomes the leader and begins sending heartbeats
The candidate must wait for the previous leader's term to expire before assuming leadership
The votes are discarded if any follower is still committing previous entries
The candidate enters a transition phase where it cannot serve requests
questions.explanation: Upon receiving a quorum of votes, a candidate becomes leader and immediately begins sending heartbeats to establish authority. Option 1 is incorrect; there is no waiting period. Option 2 assumes leaders can exist concurrently, violating safety.

questions.q: In consensus algorithms, what is meant by 'eventual consistency'?
options: Non-leader nodes may temporarily differ from the leader but will eventually apply all committed entries
Some data may be permanently lost if a majority of nodes fail simultaneously
The system will always return the most recent write within one network round trip
Faulty nodes gradually become consistent through passive healing
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency means followers lag behind the leader but converge as they apply committed log entries. Option 1 describes permanent loss, not consistency. Option 2 assumes instant consistency, which consensus cannot guarantee across network delays.
